meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve can't finish projects (Beginner Comparison)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome can't finish projects. A Beginner-level Comparison using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: SaaS Pricing Strategy: Finish Your First Student Project
content.seo_description: Stop abandoning projects. Learn how to implement SaaS pricing to gain focus, build a real-world portfolio, and launch your first app using modern AI tools.
content.answer_block: The root cause of unfinished projects is a lack of scope constraints. By defining a SaaS pricing model early, you force yourself to prioritize core value over feature creep. This constraint transforms your project from a vague idea into a marketable product, ensuring you reach a deployable MVP milestone quickly.
content.intro:
The graveyard of abandoned GitHub repositories is paved with good intentions. You start with a vision of a revolutionary app, only to drown in a sea of 'nice-to-have' features, eventually losing the spark that drove you to code in the first place. This cycle isn't a failure of your skill; it's a failure of your project architecture.

To break this, you must stop building 'apps' and start building 'businesses.' By designing your SaaS pricing model before writing a single line of business logic, you anchor your development in real user value. This shift transforms your coding sessions from aimless exploration into a disciplined, outcome-focused mission that leads to a finished, portfolio-ready product.

steps.title: Define the Value Proposition
steps.description: Before coding, determine what problem you are solving. If you can't price it, you don't understand the value. Use this to identify your 'Must-Haves' for the MVP.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze this project idea and generate a 3-tier pricing strategy (Free/Pro/Enterprise) that highlights the core value proposition for a student portfolio project.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Generate a readme.md file that outlines the core value proposition and pricing tiers for my current SaaS project.'

steps.title: Architect the Subscription Schema
steps.description: Map your database schema to support subscription states. This prevents scope creep by strictly defining what data a 'Free' user vs a 'Pro' user can access.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a database schema for user subscriptions and feature gating, ensuring the logic is modular for easy implementation.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Create a Prisma schema definition that includes a User model with subscription_tier and status fields.'

steps.title: Implement Feature Gating
steps.description: Build a reusable middleware to restrict access to paid features. This is the technical backbone that forces you to stay focused on the MVP core.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a TypeScript middleware for feature gating based on user subscription levels to protect premium API endpoints.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Implement a checkUserSubscription helper function that validates access levels for restricted routes.'

steps.title: Integrate Payment Infrastructure
steps.description: Use a production-grade payment provider (like Stripe) to handle billing. This makes your project 'real' and provides a tangible goal.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a Stripe checkout session handler and a webhook listener to process subscription updates securely.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Initialize a Stripe integration script to handle one-time checkout sessions and webhook verification.'

steps.title: Deploy and Validate
steps.description: Move your project to a live environment. Seeing real users (even friends) interact with your pricing page is the ultimate motivation to finish.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a deployment checklist for a SaaS application including environment variables for billing, database connectivity, and monitoring.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Generate a Vercel deployment configuration file that includes all necessary environment variable placeholders for production.'

pro_tips: Always start with a 'Free' tier that provides genuine value; if the free version is useless, your conversion rate will be zero.
Build your pricing table as a static component first; if you can't describe the features clearly in text, you aren't ready to code the backend.
Use feature flags to hide unfinished work; this keeps your deployment branch clean and production-ready at all times.
Implement a 'Coming Soon' state for enterprise features to satisfy the 'complete' look without needing to build the complex logic immediately.
Treat your GitHub commit history like a business log; clear, feature-focused commits are more impressive to recruiters than a single massive push.
content.next_steps: Once your pricing logic is live, add a simple analytics dashboard to track how many users click on your Pro tier—this is the first step toward real-world product iteration.
content.conversion_block: You now have the framework to turn your ideas into finished products that recruiters and users actually care about. Stop wandering and start shipping. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
